does: This paper evaluates how well LLM-based tutoring systems resist adversarial student attacks designed to extract complete answers rather than pedagogical scaffolding. The authors adapt six groups of adversarial and persuasive techniques to the educational domain and evaluate multiple tutor architectures including different model families, pedagogically-aligned variants, and multi-agent designs. They create in-context adversarial student agents and develop a fine-tuned agent specifically designed to jailbreak tutors into answer leakage, measuring the robustness of each tutor configuration under these systematic attacks. The work identifies significant vulnerabilities in current LLM tutors, demonstrating that even pedagogically-aligned models can be manipulated into bypassing their safety constraints.
connects: This work extends research on answer leakage in LLM tutoring systems, which previously evaluated pedagogical quality assuming cooperative students, by introducing an adversarial threat model. It builds on broader research in LLM safety and robustness against jailbreaking attacks, adapting adversarial attack techniques from the security literature to the educational domain. The paper connects to work on multi-agent interactions with language models and explores how principles from adversarial machine learning apply to educational AI systems. It engages with pedagogical theory around guided discovery and scaffolding while examining the tension between LLM helpfulness and educational best practices.
enables: This work establishes answer leakage robustness as a critical evaluation metric for educational AI systems and demonstrates the need for defenses against adversarial students. It opens directions for developing tutoring systems that maintain pedagogical integrity under adversarial pressure, potentially through better prompting strategies, architectural constraints, or auxiliary classifiers to detect jailbreak attempts. The systematic evaluation framework could support future research on robust pedagogical AI, informing the design of LLM tutors deployed in real educational settings where student motivation might conflict with learning objectives. This work also highlights broader questions about balancing helpfulness with appropriate guardrails in domain-specific AI applications.
